summary:
phase-1-scope.md section 1.2 claims that Phase 2 will 'identify' the `lead-adversarial-demand` lead 'when dispatching,' but phase-2-discover.md has no such logic. Phase 2 builds a generic agent prompt for every lead by placing the lead text into its 'assigned lead' field, then adds its own output format template (Findings / Implications / Surprises / Open Questions / Summary) on top. The adversarial lead's embedded prompt — which specifies per-question confidence vocabulary, the calibration section structure, and a reporter posture — gets wrapped inside Phase 2's generic template. The agent receives two competing output format instructions and may produce Phase 2's standard structure instead of the adversarial lead's. Issue 5's evals assert specific output structure (per-question confidence levels, calibration section) from this agent, so the mismatch is likely to cause eval failures. Fix: update Phase 2 to detect `lead-adversarial-demand` by name and pass its body as the complete agent prompt, bypassing Phase 2's template construction for this lead. Alternatively, remove the 'so Phase 2 can identify it' claim from Phase 1 and add an explicit output format override inside the embedded prompt itself.

Advisory: the adversarial lead's embedded prompt already has a resolved `## Visibility` block (interpolated from Phase 0's scope file in Phase 1). Phase 2's generic template also injects a second visibility block for every lead. For the adversarial lead this means the agent receives the same visibility value twice from two different template layers. No functional problem today, but the redundancy becomes noise once Finding 1 is fixed and the adversarial lead gets direct dispatch.

The implementation otherwise fits the architecture well. The Label Pre-Gate as an unnumbered section matches the Resume Check convention. Classification logic is inline in the phase file (no new registry or config). The scope file `## Visibility` section is both written (Phase 0) and consumed (Phase 1) — no orphaned state. The `lead-adversarial-demand` slug follows the kebab-case convention Phase 2 uses for output file naming.
